title: Basal Body Temperature Ovulation Calculator | Hacé Cuentas
description: Track your daily basal body temperature to detect the biphasic pattern that indicates ovulation. Natural fertility awareness tracking method.
seoKeywords: how to measure basal body temperature
intro: Your **basal body temperature (BBT)** is your body's resting temperature, measured **right after waking, before you get out of bed**. After [ovulation](/ovulation-fertile-days), progesterone causes a **shift of 0.3–0.5°C** that persists until your next period. Enter today's temperature and cycle day to analyze whether the pattern indicates ovulation.
keyTakeaway: Your basal body temperature rises **0.3–0.5°C** after ovulation (due to progesterone). If it stays elevated for more than **16 days**, it may indicate pregnancy.
useCases: You want to confirm whether you ovulated this cycle.
You use the symptothermal method to plan or prevent pregnancy.
You want to confirm ovulation after a positive LH test.
You track fertility and want to analyze your temperature pattern.
You suspect you're not ovulating and want data to discuss with your doctor.

fields.label: Cycle day (1 = first day of period)

dataUpdate.notes: Basal body temperature method for ovulation detection.

example.title: Example: 36.7°C on cycle day 16
example.result: A temperature of **36.7°C** on cycle day 16 suggests you've already ovulated (ovulation likely occurred around **day 14**). You're in the **early luteal phase**.

explanation:
## How Basal Body Temperature Works

Before [ovulation](/ovulation-fertile-days), your basal body temperature is lower (~36.1–36.4°C). After ovulation, **progesterone** raises it by ~0.3–0.5°C (~36.4–36.8°C). This shift creates a **biphasic pattern**.

## How to Measure It Correctly

1. Measure **immediately upon waking**, before getting out of bed, talking, or using the bathroom.
2. Use a **basal body temperature thermometer** (with 0.01°C precision).
3. Measure in the **same location** every time (mouth, vaginal, or rectal).
4. Measure at the **same time** each morning (±30 minutes).
5. Record your readings in a chart or app.

## Interpreting Your Pattern

| Pattern | Meaning |
|---|---|
| Biphasic (low → high) | Ovulation confirmed |
| Monophasic (no change) | Possible anovulatory cycle |
| High > 16 days | Possible pregnancy |
| Sharp drop | Period coming soon |

## Important Limitations

- Basal body temperature **confirms** that you already ovulated—it does not **predict** future ovulation.
- Fever, alcohol, insufficient sleep, and medications can affect your reading.
- Best used with other methods (cervical mucus observation, LH tests).

## Related Calculators

- [Ovulation](/ovulation-fertile-days) — find your fertile window.
- [Menstrual Cycle](/menstrual-cycle) — understand your cycle phases.

faq.q: What's a normal basal body temperature?
faq.a: Before ovulation, BBT typically ranges from **36.1 to 36.4°C** (97 to 97.5°F). After ovulation, it rises to **36.4–36.8°C** (97.5–98°F) or higher. Every woman's baseline is different—track your own pattern, not absolute numbers.

faq.q: How much does BBT rise after ovulation?
faq.a: BBT typically rises **0.3–0.5°C** (0.5–1°F). The increase may happen gradually over 1–2 days or suddenly. A **sustained rise for 3+ consecutive days** confirms ovulation has occurred.

faq.q: What if my temperature doesn't rise?
faq.a: A cycle without a clear temperature shift may indicate an **anovulatory cycle** (a cycle without ovulation). Occasional anovulatory cycles are normal, but if this happens frequently, talk to your doctor.

faq.q: Can elevated basal body temperature indicate pregnancy?
faq.a: Yes. If your temperature remains elevated for **more than 16 days** (longer than a typical luteal phase), it's a strong sign of pregnancy. Confirm with a pregnancy test.

A **basal body temperature thermometer** is recommended because it displays to **0.01°C precision** (like 36.45°C). Standard thermometers show only one decimal place and may miss the small 0.3°C shift.

faq.q: What can affect my basal body temperature reading?
faq.a: **Alcohol**, sleeping **fewer than 3 hours**, **fever**, certain medications, and **high stress** can all raise your BBT. Mark these factors on your chart so you know which readings need context.

faq.q: Is BBT alone reliable for contraception?
faq.a: Basal body temperature alone is **not reliable** for birth control. Combined with cervical mucus observation (the **symptothermal method**), effectiveness improves—but it requires careful daily tracking.

faq.q: How long does BBT stay elevated after ovulation?
faq.a: Your temperature typically stays elevated for about **12–14 days** (the luteal phase) before dropping as your period approaches. If it stays high beyond 16 days, pregnancy may be the cause.

faq.q: Why measure BBT immediately upon waking?
faq.a: Any activity—even getting out of bed—raises your body temperature. BBT is your **lowest resting temperature**, so measuring before any movement gives the most accurate reading for ovulation tracking.
